editableNewDialogue.text: Can you tighten this sentence in formal Australian business English, keeping it direct: "I request written confirmation that the proposed actions will permanently rectify the identified faults, in accordance with your warranty obligations."
I request written confirmation that the proposed actions will permanently rectify the identified faults, in accordance with your warranty obligations.
Make it firmer without being aggressive.
I require written confirmation that the proposed actions will permanently rectify the identified faults, in accordance with your warranty obligations.
I require your written assurance that the proposed actions will permanently rectify the identified faults, in accordance with your warranty obligations.
Also, can you correct this for grammar: "Your proposed next steps appear to involve further discussion, followed by review, which prolongs the matter unnecessarily."
Your proposed next steps appear to involve further discussion followed by a review, which unnecessarily prolongs the matter.

It can read as repetitive. Depending on the surrounding sentences, alternatives include "a lasting remedy", "a definitive fix", or "final resolution". In formal business correspondence, "definitive fix" or "final resolution" are usually the cleanest options.
Unrelated: I saw a piece saying a museum restored a 15th-century painting and found a hidden dog underneath. Is that a real thing?

Yes—discoveries like that do occur. During restoration, conservators use X-ray, infrared reflectography, and paint-layer analysis to reveal underdrawings or earlier compositions (pentimenti). It’s not uncommon to find hidden figures, objects, or animals that were painted over during revisions.

Please revise the following paragraph in Australian English, formal business tone—direct and serious. I intend to attach it to my complaint being lodged with NSW Fair Trading, so it needs to read as measured and unequivocal:

"Despite repeated assurances, the matter remains unresolved. I require written confirmation of a permanent rectification plan, including specific timeframes and accountability, in accordance with your warranty obligations."

Please revise the following paragraph in Australian English, using a formal business tone that is direct and serious. As this will be attached to my complaint being lodged with NSW Fair Trading, it must read as measured and unequivocal:

"Despite repeated assurances, the matter remains unresolved. I require written confirmation of a permanent rectification plan, including specific timeframes and accountability, in accordance with your warranty obligations."

One correction before you revise this further: I am not demanding a permanent rectification anymore. At this point, my objective is a buyback or replacement under the warranty/consumer guarantees, because the faults have continued for years and I no longer accept further repair attempts.

Understood. I will remove any wording that frames your position as seeking a permanent rectification, and instead state that you are requesting a buyback or replacement under warranty/consumer guarantees due to ongoing faults and your refusal of further repair attempts.
Correct. Please ensure the wording is direct and serious, and makes it clear that repair is no longer an acceptable outcome.

annotation.queryText: I’m drafting a short email to support about a recurring issue that keeps coming back even after they “fix” it. Without getting into the technical details, what core expectation should I clearly state I’m insisting on, and what’s the underlying justification I’d give for why they owe me that level of remedy rather than another temporary patch?

This confirmation must explicitly reference the warranty obligations LDV has repeatedly acknowledged but failed to fulfill.
